column2: # CBPS vs. MLE propensity score diagnostic — Card (1995)
#   Compare kappa weights and ESS under MLE logit vs. CBPS propensity scores
#   for both the Card and Kitagawa covariate specifications.
#   Key question: is the extreme-weight / low-ESS problem a structural overlap
#   issue (data), or does the MLE amplify it relative to CBPS?
#   - card_clean  : the cleaned Card dataset (with nearc4, somecol, educ16,
#                   all covariate columns, and Y in log-dollars)
#   - All functions from functions_card.R already sourced:
# 0.  Grab the core vectors (adjust names if yours differ)
Y  <- card_clean$lwage          # log wage in dollars — adjust if named differently
# 1.  Design matrices — exactly as in your main analysis
# 2.  Estimate propensity scores: MLE and CBPS, both specs
cat("  MLE done. Fitting CBPS (Card spec)...\n")
cat(sprintf("  CBPS Card  — converged: %s, max moment: %.2e\n",
CBPS Card  — converged: TRUE, max moment: 5.31e-15
cat(sprintf("  CBPS Kit   — converged: %s, max moment: %.2e\n\n",
CBPS Kit   — converged: TRUE, max moment: 2.29e-14
cat(sprintf("  pct above 0.93     : %.1f%%  <- the κ=-13.9 threshold\n",
# 4.  Kappa weights: MLE vs CBPS, Card vs Kitagawa, D1 vs D2
kappa_diag(Z, D1, p_card_ml, "D1=somecol | Card spec  | MLE")
kappa_diag(Z, D1, p_card_cb, "D1=somecol | Card spec  | CBPS")
kappa_diag(Z, D2, p_card_ml, "D2=educ16  | Card spec  | MLE")
kappa_diag(Z, D2, p_card_cb, "D2=educ16  | Card spec  | CBPS")
#     using your exact kappa_outcome_weights() + weight_diag() functions
run_diag(D1, p_card_ml, "tau_u   | D1 somecol | Card  | MLE",
run_diag(D1, p_card_cb, "tau_u   | D1 somecol | Card  | CBPS",
run_diag(D1, p_kit_ml,  "tau_u   | D1 somecol | Kit   | MLE",
run_diag(D1, p_kit_cb,  "tau_u   | D1 somecol | Kit   | CBPS",
run_diag(D2, p_card_ml, "tau_u   | D2 educ16  | Card  | MLE",
run_diag(D2, p_card_cb, "tau_u   | D2 educ16  | Card  | CBPS",
run_diag(D2, p_kit_ml,  "tau_u   | D2 educ16  | Kit   | MLE",
run_diag(D2, p_kit_cb,  "tau_u   | D2 educ16  | Kit   | CBPS",
# 6.  The "risk cell" diagnostic: high p, Z=0, D=1 — MLE vs CBPS
#     Shows whether CBPS shrinks the extreme propensity values that cause
cat("RISK CELL: high p, observed Z=0, D=1  (the κ extreme source)\n")
RISK CELL: high p, observed Z=0, D=1  (the κ extreme source)
risk_cell_diag <- function(p, D, Z, kappa_vec, label, cutoffs = c(0.70, 0.80, 0.90)) {
cat(sprintf("  p > %.2f: 0 obs in risk cell\n", cut))
cat(sprintf("  p > %.2f: N=%3d | min(κ)=%7.2f | mean(κ)=%7.2f | max(p)=%.4f\n",
risk_cell_diag(p_card_ml, D1, Z, kw_d1_card_ml, "D1=somecol | Card spec  | MLE")
risk_cell_diag(p_card_cb, D1, Z, kw_d1_card_cb, "D1=somecol | Card spec  | CBPS")
risk_cell_diag(p_kit_ml,  D1, Z, kw_d1_kit_ml,  "D1=somecol | Kitagawa   | MLE")
risk_cell_diag(p_kit_cb,  D1, Z, kw_d1_kit_cb,  "D1=somecol | Kitagawa   | CBPS")
# 7.  Point estimates for tau_u: all four combinations
#     So you can see whether CBPS also changes the estimate, not just the weights
cat("POINT ESTIMATES: tau_u across specs and p-score methods\n")
POINT ESTIMATES: tau_u across specs and p-score methods
